text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY
SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE
18 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT

ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
Department: Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Survivorship

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Routine 18-month post-treatment surveillance for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 53-year-old never-smoking woman of East Asian descent with a history of p16-positive, HPV-16-confirmed T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, stage III, HPV-positive staging) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, who presents today for her 18-month post-treatment surveillance visit.

To briefly summarize the pertinent history: the patient was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with a single left level IIA metastatic lymph node (2.1 cm), confirmed on direct laryngoscopy biopsy and FNA cytology. Molecular profile was notable for HPV-16 positivity on ISH, p16 overexpression on IHC, PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation (VAF 28%), TRAF3 truncating mutation, KMT2D loss-of-function mutation, and FBXW7 mutation; TP53 was wild-type consistent with the HPV-positive phenotype. TMB was intermediate at 7.2 mutations/Mb, PD-L1 CPS 18. Given these genomic findings and the T2N1 disease burden, enrollment on a de-escalation protocol was discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board but ultimately not pursued; standard curative-intent definitive IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary site and gross nodal disease, with elective nodal irradiation bilaterally) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks was recommended and administered.

The treatment course was complicated by significant toxicity: she developed grade 3 confluent oropharyngeal mucositis by approximately week 5 of chemoradiation, requiring PEG tube placement for nutritional support, as well as grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.32 mg/dL from baseline 0.78 mg/dL) that resulted in omission of the third cisplatin cycle. She received two of three planned cisplatin cycles (cumulative dose 340 mg/m2). Radiation was completed without interruption at full prescribed dose. She subsequently achieved a complete metabolic response on 12-week post-treatment PET-CT (SUVmax at primary site declined from 12.4 to 1.8; left level IIA node down to 0.9 cm with no pathologic FDG avidity). PEG tube was removed approximately 8 weeks after completing treatment. 6-month and 12-month surveillance imaging showed no recurrent disease; the 12-month PET-CT was entirely clear.

At today's 18-month surveillance visit, Ms. Chen reports she is feeling extremely well overall. She has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher, which she finds gratifying and reports as a meaningful marker of her functional recovery. She denies any new throat discomfort, odynophagia, dysphagia, hoarseness, otalgia, or sensation of a lump in the throat. She is tolerating a completely normal diet without restriction. She notes that her swallowing has essentially normalized and she no longer has to modify food consistency or pace of eating. She reports persistent, mild xerostomia — her primary ongoing complaint — which she manages with frequent water sips throughout the day and occasional use of Biotene oral rinse. She describes the dry mouth as mildly bothersome but not significantly impairing her quality of life or ability to work or socialize.

Regarding her thyroid: she was diagnosed with post-radiation hypothyroidism at the 12-month visit (TSH 7.1 mIU/L, free T4 0.8 ng/dL) and started on levothyroxine 50 mcg daily. Her dose was uptitrated to 75 mcg daily at a subsequent visit, and she reports feeling well on this regimen — no fatigue, no cold intolerance, no constipation or other hypothyroid symptoms. She is compliant with her medication.

She denies any new neurological symptoms, weight loss, hemoptysis, dyspnea, or bone pain. No new skin lesions or palpable masses noted by the patient. ECOG performance status 0.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Mild xerostomia (chronic, stable, improving compared to prior visits). Reports occasional mild neck tightness/stiffness, particularly in the anterior neck, consistent with known post-radiation fibrosis.
Negative: No sore throat, no odynophagia, no dysphagia, no voice change or hoarseness, no otalgia, no epistaxis, no hemoptysis, no trismus or jaw pain, no new skin changes, no lymphadenopathy palpable by patient, no fatigue, no unintentional weight loss, no fevers or chills, no nausea or vomiting, no peripheral neuropathy symptoms (had grade 1 cisplatin-related fingertip tingling post-treatment, now fully resolved), no tinnitus, no hearing difficulty (had mild grade 1 high-frequency audiometric loss post-treatment; no subjective functional impairment), no shortness of breath, no chest pain, no abdominal symptoms, no bone pain.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (post-radiation hypothyroidism)
- Biotene dry mouth rinse PRN (xerostomia)
- Artificial saliva spray (OTC) PRN
- Multivitamin daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 14/min, Temp 36.8 C, SpO2 98% on room air.
Weight: 59 kg (up approximately 1 kg from last visit 6 months ago; pre-treatment baseline was 62 kg; patient remains approximately 3 kg below pre-treatment weight, stable and acceptable).

General: Alert, pleasant woman in no acute distress. Well-appearing and well-nourished. Conversational voice is clear and strong with no dysphonia.

Head/Face: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No facial asymmetry. Trismus absent — mouth opens to approximately 3.5 cm interincisal distance (normal; no interval change).

Oral cavity/oropharynx: Mucosa of the oral cavity is moist with mild residual xerostomia appreciated clinically (reduced salivary pooling under tongue). No oral candidiasis. Dentition in reasonable condition; patient has been followed by her dentist regularly. Mild post-radiation mucosal pallor of the posterior oropharynx noted, consistent with expected post-treatment changes.

Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic today): Nasopharynx clear. Soft palate and uvula with normal mobility. Oropharynx and base of tongue mucosa appears smooth, healed, and without any visible lesion, asymmetry, exophytic mass, or mucosal irregularity. Specifically, the left base of tongue is flat and smooth with no evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. Vallecular and epiglottic regions normal. Piriform sinuses symmetric. Bilateral true and false vocal cords with normal appearance and full mobility. No subglottic or hypopharyngeal abnormality. Findings consistent with complete clinical remission.

Neck: Supple. Mild palpable fibrosis of the bilateral anterior and lateral neck soft tissues consistent with post-radiation changes, stable compared to prior examination. No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No masses. No carotid bruits.

Skin (neck): Hyperpigmentation and mild skin texture change in the bilateral anterior neck from prior radiation field, unchanged.

Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No palpable lymphadenopathy in the axillae.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC DATA:
TSH: 1.9 mIU/L (normal; improved and within target range on levothyroxine 75 mcg). Free T4: pending (ordered today for annual thyroid function assessment).
CBC: WBC 5.8 k/uL, Hgb 13.4 g/dL, platelets 224 k/uL — all within normal limits.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139, potassium 4.1, creatinine 0.81 mg/dL (near baseline; renal function fully recovered), BUN 14, glucose 92, albumin 4.2 g/dL — unremarkable.

No surveillance imaging ordered at this visit per the established surveillance plan (imaging to be performed annually, with the next CT or PET-CT anticipated at the 24-month mark per schedule).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (p16+, HPV-16, left base of tongue, T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition Stage III), s/p definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT:
   - Patient continues in complete clinical remission at 18 months post-treatment. Nasopharyngoscopy today confirms no evidence of locoregional recurrence. No clinical or symptomatic concern for distant disease.
   - The 18-month milestone in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC is highly favorable. Published data consistently demonstrate that the vast majority of HPV-positive OPSCC patients who have achieved complete response at 12 weeks and remained disease-free at 12 months continue to be cured; late recurrences are uncommon, though not impossible, particularly with the PIK3CA co-mutation noted in her tumor profile.
   - Continue biannual clinical surveillance (examination every 6 months). Next clinic visit in 6 months for 24-month surveillance examination.
   - CT neck with contrast planned for 24-month visit per surveillance schedule. PET-CT not required at this interval in the absence of clinical concern.
   - Patient instructed on signs/symptoms of recurrence: new neck mass, progressive sore throat or dysphagia, referred otalgia, hoarseness, or new constitutional symptoms — should contact clinic immediately if any of these develop rather than waiting for scheduled visit.

2. Post-radiation hypothyroidism:
   - TSH now 1.9 mIU/L — well-controlled and within target range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Will continue current dose without adjustment.
   - Annual TSH and free T4 monitoring; free T4 ordered today.
   - Patient aware to call if she develops symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism.

3. Post-radiation xerostomia (grade 1, stable/improving):
   - Mild, managed adequately with non-pharmacologic measures (Biotene, artificial saliva, frequent hydration). Not significantly impairing quality of life or function.
   - No intervention required at this time. Xerostomia often continues to very slowly improve up to 2-3 years post-treatment, particularly given the bilateral parotid sparing achieved with the IMRT technique (mean parotid dose ~24 Gy).
   - Encouraged to maintain good oral hygiene and continue regular dental visits given ongoing radiation-related xerostomia risk and caries prevention.

4. Post-radiation fibrosis (neck):
   - Mild, stable anterior and lateral neck fibrosis noted on exam. No functional limitation.
   - Encouraged to continue range-of-motion neck exercises and soft tissue massage as previously instructed by physical therapy.
   - No additional intervention at this time; referral to PT/lymphedema specialist offered if symptoms become problematic.

5. Survivorship/general health:
   - Patient is returning to full-time teaching — excellent functional recovery.
   - She remains engaged in HPV vaccination advocacy in her school community, which is strongly encouraged; counseled on the importance of HPV vaccination for adolescents and unvaccinated young adults in her circle.
   - Screening mammography and other age-appropriate preventive health measures discussed; patient reports is up to date.
   - ECOG PS 0. Continue supportive/survivorship care.

Return to clinic in 6 months for 24-month post-treatment surveillance. Patient verbalized understanding and has no additional questions at this time. She expressed gratitude for her care team and for the outcome achieved.

Time spent with patient: approximately 40 minutes, including flexible nasopharyngoscopy and extensive counseling.

Electronically signed: [Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
